skill.name: paypal-integration
skill.description: Integrate PayPal payment processing with support for express checkout, subscriptions, and refund management. Use when implementing PayPal payments, processing online transactions, or building e-commerce checkout flows.
skill.summary: Build PayPal checkout, subscription, IPN, refund, and sandbox testing flows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static external command and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown text and fenced code examples. The confirmed risk is legitimate network-facing payment code that uses PayPal APIs and local endpoints, with simplified examples that need production hardening.

medium_findings.title: Network Payment Examples Require Production Controls
medium_findings.description: The skill includes copyable examples that call PayPal OAuth, checkout, subscription, refund, and IPN endpoints. These calls are appropriate for a PayPal integration skill, but they process payment data and credentials, so implementations need secure secret storage, request validation, timeouts, and error handling before production use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples directly perform outbound requests to PayPal API endpoints. The context is legitimate payment integration guidance, but payment and credential handling raises real implementation risk.

medium_findings.title: Simplified IPN Processing Guidance
medium_findings.description: The IPN example verifies messages with PayPal and checks duplicate transaction IDs, but it leaves important production checks as comments. A production implementation should validate receiver account, currency, amount, item identity, transaction state, replay handling, logging, and persistence before fulfillment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example has an authenticity check, but the surrounding business validation is incomplete. This is a common documentation simplification rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged backtick and code fence markers as Ruby or shell execution. These lines are Markdown examples and instructions to open a local resource, not executable code run by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside Markdown fences or inline Markdown. There is no script file, shell invocation, subprocess call, or dynamic execution path in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography alert points to the skill description line. That line describes PayPal payment processing and contains no cryptographic algorithm, implementation, or security-sensitive code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is natural language metadata, not cryptographic code. No weak algorithm use is evidenced at that location.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Identifier False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain payment identifiers such as client_id, txn_id, parent_txn_id, and reason_code. They do not query hostnames, operating system data, users, network interfaces, or local system configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is payment transaction handling. No evidence found of host or environment reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are PayPal And Placeholder Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to PayPal SDK and API endpoints or placeholder return and cancel URLs. They are expected for examples, but users must configure environment-specific domains and avoid mixing sandbox and production settings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains and placeholder URLs match the stated PayPal integration purpose. The risk is configuration misuse, not evidence of malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Credential Placeholders Must Not Become Hardcoded Secrets
low_findings.description: The examples show client ID and secret placeholders inline. They are not real secrets, but marketplace users should store PayPal credentials in a secret manager or environment configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders, so no credential leak is present. The pattern is still worth documenting because users may copy examples into production code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser-To-Backend Capture Request
dangerous_patterns.description: The frontend example sends an order ID to a local capture endpoint. This pattern is valid only when the backend independently verifies order ownership, amount, currency, and payer state before capture.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code shows a common PayPal flow, but the security of the pattern depends on backend validation that is not fully shown in the snippet.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Requests Without Explicit Timeouts
dangerous_patterns.description: Several Python requests examples omit explicit timeout values. Production payment services should set connection and read timeouts to avoid resource exhaustion and delayed transaction handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The requests calls are visible and no timeout argument is shown. This is a reliability and resilience concern rather than malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Add PayPal Checkout, Subscriptions, and Refunds
content.value_statement: PayPal payment work often spans checkout, subscriptions, IPN messages, refunds, and sandbox testing. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for building and reviewing those flows.
actual_capabilities: Explains PayPal Checkout and Smart Payment Button setup.
Shows server-side order creation, capture, and order lookup patterns.
Provides IPN verification and payment event handling guidance.
Covers recurring billing through plans and subscriptions.
Outlines refund creation, refund lookup, and sandbox testing workflows.
limitations: Does not include a complete production application or database layer.
Uses simplified examples that require security hardening before launch.
Does not replace current PayPal documentation or compliance review.
Does not provide real credentials, live merchant setup, or legal advice.

use_cases.title: Launch PayPal Checkout
use_cases.description: Add a PayPal payment option to an ecommerce checkout with frontend approval and backend capture.

use_cases.title: Process Payment Notifications
use_cases.description: Design an IPN handler that verifies PayPal messages before fulfillment or account updates.

use_cases.title: Manage Recurring Billing
use_cases.description: Plan subscription products, create billing plans, and route customers to PayPal approval.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Checkout Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PayPal integration skill to outline a sandbox checkout flow for a single product. Include frontend, backend, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review Payment Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PayPal integration skill to review my order creation and capture flow. Check amount validation, currency handling, duplicate processing, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design Subscription Billing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PayPal integration skill to design a subscription billing flow with plan creation, approval routing, cancellation handling, and failed payment handling.

prompt_templates.title: Harden IPN And Refunds
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PayPal integration skill to create a production hardening checklist for IPN verification, refunds, chargebacks, logging, idempotency, and sandbox-to-live migration.

output_examples.input: Create a PayPal checkout plan for a digital course.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step implementation plan covering Smart Payment Buttons, backend order capture, server-side amount validation, sandbox testing, and launch checks.

output_examples.input: Review my IPN handler before launch.
output: Verify each IPN with PayPal before processing.
Reject mismatched receiver account, currency, amount, or item values.
Make fulfillment idempotent by transaction ID.
Log payment states and failures for audit review.

output_examples.input: Add refund support for captured payments.
output_examples.output: A refund workflow with capture ID lookup, optional partial refunds, payer notes, status tracking, and staff-visible error handling.

best_practices: Keep PayPal credentials in secure configuration, not source files.
Validate payment amount, currency, merchant account, and transaction state on the server.
Test checkout, IPN, refunds, disputes, and subscriptions in sandbox before live use.
anti_patterns: Do not fulfill orders from client-side success callbacks alone.
Do not process IPN messages without PayPal verification and duplicate checks.
Do not mix sandbox and production credentials or endpoint URLs.

faq.question: Does this skill implement PayPal for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides integration guidance and examples that a developer must adapt and test.

faq.question: Can it help with subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers subscription plans, subscriber creation, approval URLs, and recurring billing concerns.

faq.question: Does it cover refunds?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes refund creation, refund detail lookup, and refund workflow considerations.

faq.question: Is the example code production ready?
faq.answer: No. The examples need secure secrets, validation, timeouts, persistence, logging, and compliance review.

faq.question: Does it support PayPal sandbox testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends sandbox credentials and test accounts before live payment processing.

faq.question: Is there confirmed malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The confirmed risk is legitimate payment network activity that requires careful implementation.
